Follow that with a sheet of plywood, a secondhand transmitter, a dab of epoxy and at least one orbiting satellite. By Jon Little Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 19, 2001) Soldotna -- The Kenai Peninsula's overall moose numbers are down and are likely to stay that way for years, according to wildlife biologists. State Area Management biologist Ted Spraker recently sat in the back seat of a Piper Super Cub flying over the lakes and low hills north of Sterling. He was counting the moose he saw in one of the richest habitats on the Peninsula but said there are 30 percent fewer bulls than there were three years ago. The moose population in that area is shrinking and is down from a high of 3,500 in the 1980s to an estimated 2,000 now, he said. The reasons are twofold. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Research doesn't support listing of sea otters ENDANGERED SPECIES: Population decline could be blamed on predation. By Laine Welch Fisheries (Published: November 17, 2001) Kodiak -- The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service has decided that sea otters along the Aleutian Islands do not warrant being listed as depleted under the Marine Mammal Protection Act. However, the species could still be listed as threatened or endangered under the Endangered Species Act. Any listing holds the potential to constrain fishing, because of increased regulations and area closures. Sea otter populations throughout the Aleutians have dropped about 70 percent in the past decade, researchers say. title: Native language program nets grant
summary: The students at the elementary school in Arctic Village understand the Gwich'in language because they hear it at home. But they don't learn it in school. Caroline Tritt-Frank, who teaches the 26 kids at the school this year, wants to change that with help from a fledgling University of Alaska Fairbanks program that just received a $1 million federal grant. Tritt-Frank expects to earn a master's degree next month in Native language education, specializing in the Gwich'in language. For six years, she taught standard subjects in Gwich'in, mixing in traditional information gleaned from talks with elders. Disappointing standardized test scores for Arctic Village students prompted the school to end the language immersion program this year to focus on reading in English, Frank said. But in the fu…

title: ANCHORAGEWelder dies at airport in construction accident
summary: ANCHORAGE Welder dies at airport in construction accident A welder fell to his death while working on a construction project Wednesday morning at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport. Airport police said Bill Erhard, 39, fell about 90 feet. The accident occurred at 7:29 a.m. as Erhard worked on Concourse C. Mark Butler, airport spokesman, said Erhard worked for Kiewit Construction Co., which is doing steelwork for the new airport terminal. Project superintendent Michael Barta of Kiewit could not be reached for comment Wednesday evening. Erhard's father, Donald, said his son was a member of Ironworkers Local 751 and had worked in the industry for the last 15 years. He grew up in Kansas and California and followed his dad to Alaska five years ago. Erhard worked for four or five compan…

title: Decline in Kodiak's otter population worries experts
summary: Kodiak -- A survey conducted this summer indicates that Kodiak's otter population is down 40 percent. Angie Doroff of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service said the agency is still seeking supplemental information before the results are finalized. But she expects adjustments to the overall estimate to be slight. The magnitude of the decline is worrisome, Doroff said. The Kodiak survey was part of an effort to determine the eastern extent of sea otter declines. Surveys in the Aleutian Islands last year show otter populations in that area have dropped 70 to 90 percent since the 1970s. Kodiak sea otters are primarily descendants of a group of otters from Shuyak Island that survived massive fur-trade hunts in the 19th century. Most otters in the Kodiak archipelago live around Shuyak and Afognak I…

title: Suit stalls Hatcher Pass ski plans
summary: Wasilla -- The long-dreamed-of ski area at Hatcher Pass has landed again the center of a Mat-Su political battle. A group of former borough officials is suing the current administration to stop the borough from purchasing a lease to develop 11,000 acres surrounding Government Peak. Friends of Hatcher Pass, which includes a former borough mayor, manager and assemblyman, filed the lawsuit last week in Superior Court in Palmer. Specifically, they accuse borough officials of withholding public documents, including an appraisal of the value of the lease, and illegally holding closed meetings to discuss buying the lease from the private developer who currently holds it. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: September 5, 2001) A first-ever "listening conference" for top Native health managers in Anchorage to hear from patients brought out compliments, suggestions and some serious concerns that health organizations now are trying to address. Thirty-three patients either spoke out or wrote in comments at the daylong session Aug. 24 at Southcentral Foundation's gathering room on the campus of Alaska Native Medical Center, according to a summary provided by the organization. title: TRADITION: Salvation Army, Alaska National Guard deliver holiday cheer to remote villages. DT: 11/17/01
summary: St. George -- Santa Claus flew 670 miles in a droning C-130 Hercules to deliver toys and Christmas dinners to this dot of land in the Bering Sea on Friday. A good share of the Aleut village -- more than 100 people -- turned out on a chilly, rainy afternoon to meet him in the school gym, home of the St. George Seal Pups. Kids ripped into wrapped toys and boogied on the gym floor to carols sung by members of the Air Force Band while parents and grandparents visited with each other and the planeload of volunteers and Guard members who spent four hours in the village. Operation Santa Claus, a 44-year-old partnership between the Alaska National Guard and the Salvation Army, delivered on its mission to ferry toys, books and clothing to children in rural Alaska. title: Cruise lines to pay fines of $402,500
summary: Juneau -- The state will collect nearly half a million dollars from seven cruise lines for air-quality violations in Southeast Alaska. The state Department of Environmental Conservation announced Thursday that it has concluded all enforcement actions on alleged air opacity violations from last year and most of this year. Air opacity refers to the degree to which smokestack emissions reduce visibility or create a haze. They are distinguished from ambient-air violations, of which there were none, that involve excessive emissions of such pollutants as sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides and fine particulates. summary: Too often neglected, the ballot box needs to get more attention from Alaska Natives if they want to control their future, community leaders said Friday at the Alaska Federation of Natives convention. By Dan Joling The Associated Press (Published: October 29, 2001) Alaska ivory carvers found bargains at a government clearance sale Friday in Anchorage. Three agencies sold an estimated 500 pounds of salvaged walrus tusks to Alaska Natives eager to turn them into artwork, carvings and jewelry. "I'm going to use it in my art. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game and the Eskimo Walrus Commission co-sponsored the ivory sale. summary: | | | Akutaq is star attraction at Spirit of Denali event ESKIMO ICE CREAM You see home when you see this.' By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 24, 2001) People lined up Tuesday for the cool and rich akutaq that Louise Leonard spooned into paper cups at Brother Francis Shelter -- and got something more than dessert. As she's done for years, Leonard prepared a gigantic bowl of what Westerners call Eskimo ice cream for the shelter's annual Spirit of Denali event honoring Native culture. title: CRIME STOPPERSAnchorage police are looking for help in finding missing woman
summary: CRIME STOPPERS Anchorage police are looking for help in finding missing woman Anchorage police are asking for help in finding a 20-year-old woman who has been missing since March. Lisa Marie McCumiskey was last seen on or about March 22. According to witnesses, McCumiskey had been to Chilkoot Charlie's on the night she disappeared. Afterward, she called a friend from a pay phone at Sicily's Pizza on Lake Otis Parkway. McCumiskey never made it home and has not been seen or heard from since then. She is 5 feet 10 inches tall, and weighs 130 pounds. She has blonde hair and green eyes. If you have information about McCumiskey's location, call Crime Stoppers at 561-7867. title: IRS urges action to claim rebates
summary: Nearly 1,100 Alaskans who did not receive their tax rebate checks are being urged to contact the Internal Revenue Service to claim their checks. The IRS began issuing the one-time tax rebate checks in July, but some were returned by the postal service as undeliverable, IRS spokeswoman Judy Monahan said Tuesday. Most of the checks came back because they could not be mailed to the proper taxpayer. That occurs commonly when taxpayers move to a new address or change last names when they get married. Monahan said people who don't call the IRS by Dec. 5 will be forced to wait for their rebate money until they file their 2001 income tax returns next year. The total for the returned checks for Alaska taxpayers is approximately $367,700, or an average of $336 per check. The IRS needs to get the cor…

title: HEARING Prosecutor says waste, theft could cost taxpayers millions.
summary: Wasilla -- Unscrupulous doctors and other medical providers could easily cheat Alaska's Medicaid program through fraudulent or inflated billings, and the problem of waste and fraud may cost Alaska taxpayers tens of millions of dollars each year, a prosecutor told a legislative subcommittee Thursday. "It is a very lucrative way to steal money," said Steve Branchflower, who has directed the state Medicaid Fraud Control Unit since 1998. Medicaid is a fast-growing program that provides insurance coverage to poor people as well as children and pregnant women in middle-income families. Last fiscal year, it served 118,000 Alaskans and cost $581 million, split between the state and federal governments. title: Owner again accused of neglect
summary: Sterling -- Alaska State Troopers are threatening to seize at least 45 dogs owned by a Sterling woman who has been accused of animal abuse for the last year. Troopers said they found four dead dogs, two dead cats and scores of dogs needing basic care during a tour on Monday of a wooded lot where Sterling resident Caroline Boughton keeps her animals. They set an ultimatum. "She's got till Friday to make living conditions for those dogs better, or we will seize the dogs," said Trooper 1st Sgt. Charles Tressler. As many as 20 dogs, purebred bouvier des Flandres and Kerry blue terriers, were kenneled in double-decker plywood pens inside a ramshackle Greyhound-style bus that was so fouled by urine and feces that the ammonia-like stench nearly brought tears to the eyes of veterinarian Jerry Nyba…

title: Activist sues state, wants to create Non-Partisan Party for unaligned voters
summary: Juneau -- An activist is suing the state to create a party for the traditionally large segment of Alaskans whose political persuasion is "none of the above." The Non-Partisan Party would be a home for Alaska voters disenfranchised by the six officially recognized political parties. Since more than half of the 457,000 registered voters in Alaska fall into this category, the party could one day be a sizable electoral block, said the group's founder, Bob Allen. "It's not a wacko movement," Allen said. "There are more unaligned voters in the state of Alaska than all other parties combined." The Non-Partisan Party believes all issues and candidates should be examined without regard to political affiliation. title: Community remembers elder
summary: Fairbanks -- Effie Kokrine spent the last day of her life singing, dancing, telling jokes and laughing a lot with other elders in Tanana, an Athabaskan village at the confluence of the Yukon and Tanana rivers. After a short nap late Thursday night and a game of solitaire, Kokrine, 82, a dog musher and beloved educator in the Fairbanks School District, died in her sleep Friday at the Tanana Elders residence overlooking the Yukon River she so loved. "She had a great day," said daughter Annette Freiburger. Kokrine was in Tanana to attend the first-anniversary memorial potlatch for Elizabeth "Pinky" Folger, her sister-in-law. title: AT&T to scrap wireless services
summary: Ten months after AT&T Wireless Services launched an air attack on the Anchorage telephone and Internet market, it is scrapping the wireless service nationally at a cost of $1.3 billion. The company said its fixed-based wireless network, an innovative service that beams local phone and high-speed Internet into homes, no longer fits with its core cellphone business. The idea of using cell towers to beam phone and data into homes, rather than transmit signals over phone lines and cable, was born four years ago when AT&T Wireless was part of telecom mogul AT&T Corp. After AT&T Wireless became its own business this year, the company lost enthusiasm for fixed-base wireless, said company spokesman David Hale. By Mike Chambers The Associated Press (Published: November 6, 2001) Juneau -- Alaska North Slope crude continued its downward slide Monday, trading below $18 a barrel for the first time since July 1999. In New York trading, Alaska crude delivered to the West Coast brought $17.69 a barrel at its close, down 15 cents from Friday. North Slope oil prices have sputtered along with world prices of crude because of global economic activity compounded by the September terrorist attacks. It has caused some consternation among state economists in Alaska, where oil revenue makes up 65 percent of the state's general fund. summary: | | | IRS urges action to claim rebates ALASKANS: Many tax refund checks were undeliverable. The Associated Press (Published: October 31, 2001) Nearly 1,100 Alaskans who did not receive their tax rebate checks are being urged to contact the Internal Revenue Service to claim their checks. The IRS began issuing the one-time tax rebate checks in July, but some were returned by the postal service as undeliverable, IRS spokeswoman Judy Monahan said Tuesday. title: Climate shifts studied
summary: Homer -- There's El Nino, the warming shift that hits the Pacific for a year or two. There's the Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the temperature flip-flop that heats or cools the ocean every 20 to 40 years. And there's the general warming trend of global climate, the effects of which are more pronounced in the polar latitudes. Scientists are still trying to understand how each of those mechanisms work. But they're going to have to understand how they all work in tandem to make accurate predictions about future climate change and its impacts on everything from salmon runs to sea lion survival. title: Bull moose runs over children
summary: Two children trick-or-treating with their father Wednesday evening in South Anchorage were run over by a bull moose the family may have spooked. The children, 9-year-old Lydia Forbes and her 6-year-old brother, Taylor, were not seriously hurt, said their mother, Christine Forbes. "He had a bloody nose, and she has a bruise on her back," Forbes said. "My husband was with them and just about got it too." Paramedics examined the girl and boy at the scene and released them to their parents, said Dave Dodds, a paramedic battalion chief with the Anchorage Fire Department. "Evidently the kids and their father were walking across their front yard and they spooked up a moose," Dodds said. "It bowled them over. . . . She has a minor scrape on her back -- that's about it." By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published: October 1, 2001) Alakanuk -- In a little-known role, the Alaska National Guard is backing law enforcement agencies in the struggle against drugs across the state. Committing troops, weapons and technology, the Guard has teamed with the agencies to interdict drug shipments and stop drug production. It visits schools to educate kids about the dangers of substance abuse. And it brings what resources it can to fighting two Alaska nightmares: alcohol abuse and huffing. summary: In Alaska Outdoors News Grizzly attack By Jan Thacker Daily News Correspondent (Published: September 23, 2001) North Pole Time has begun to heal the patchwork quilt of stitches that laced Johnny McCoy's scalp and arms back together, but hunting buddy Gary Corle can still hear the agony of McCoy's screams and the crunch of his bones breaking in the jaws of a grizzly bear several weeks ago. That the North Pole pastor survived the bear attack, the hunting buddies attribute to God and luck. That McCoy lived to tell his tale, however, is a testimony to friendship, to the will of one man to see his buddy safe. title: Terror attacks hamper halibut market
summary: The East Coast terror attacks have sent cross-continent ripples through the Alaska seafood industry, hurting the halibut fishery in particular. Fishermen and processors say prices at the dock for the big flatfish have dropped by 15 percent or more, in part because of transportation delays but also because of reduced demand for halibut in restaurants and supermarkets. "Halibut is a luxury food, an expensive fish," said Brad Faulkner of Alaska Custom Seafoods in Homer, a major halibut buyer. "In any recession or downturn or malaise like you're seeing after the attacks, people draw in their belts and don't buy luxury foods." title: ATVs restricted in Snow River basin
summary: Seward -- Hunters who travel through the Snow River drainage each autumn in search of moose will have to leave their all-terrain vehicles behind. The south fork of Snow River has a small run of silver and red salmon, and the state Department of Fish and Game doesn't want vehicles disturbing spawning beds or washing out fingerlings. While the move is not intended to deter hunters, some don't like the idea of making their way through the country on foot or with horses. The main point of access to the site is from a highway pullout near Mile 13 of the Seward Highway, adjacent to the Grayling Lake trail head. title: CSED converting computers
summary: Alaska's child support agency is shutting down its system for processing payments Friday evening to convert to a new computer program. Checks normally mailed out the first of the month will be delayed until Aug. 3. Workers won't have access to case information Monday and Tuesday but will be able to provide some information Aug. 1, with full operations restored Aug. 2, according to the Alaska Child Support Enforcement Division. Parents also won't be able to get updates through the automated KIDS Line starting Friday until the end of next week. The new system will provide easier to read monthly statements and account summaries for parents, said Barbara Miklos, CSED director. title: First snowfall doesn't officially measure up
summary: All that snow that fell overnight and Thursday morning? The five inches that blanketed east Anchorage? The foot that dumped on the upper Hillside? The slippery white goop that sent cars into ditches across the Anchorage Bowl? It never happened. Not officially anyway. Snow falls for the record only when instruments at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport catch enough of it to measure, and hardly a trace fell at the airport and other parts of West Anchorage, said meteorologist Dan Keirns. "Officially, we're still waiting for our first measurable snow of the year in Anchorage, and that's what the climatological record will show," Keirns said Thursday afternoon. title: Costco boots high school lunch crowd
summary: The South Anchorage Costco has booted Dimond High School students from the store's cafe, saying they have caused too many customer complaints about rude behavior. Staff members at Costco on Dimond Boulevard are checking membership cards at the exit doors at lunchtime to keep Dimond students out, said Blaine Boyle, a store supervisor. Profit from students who regularly buy pizza, soda and hot dogs isn't worth the grief some teens are causing Costco members, he said. "What happened is, every day at lunchtime, the kids from Dimond High come in here loud, noisy, making a mess and being very rude to the people that were in here with membership buying groceries and eating in the food court," Boyle said. Too many customers have complained about students cussing and cutting in lines, he said. Cost…

title: Group rebukes UA president
summary: University of Alaska President Mark Hamilton's strong opinions on the war against terrorism are drawing fire from a group of students and faculty members who favor nonviolence. Critics claim the retired Army general's remarks supporting military action should be viewed as his own and not those of university faculty members, staffers or students. Hamilton wrote a letter on Sept. 11 that he said he composed minutes after watching televised accounts of terrorists crashing hijacked jets into the World Trade Center. The letter was published the next day as an opinion piece in the Daily News.
